title: Return to Work and Modified Duties
synonyms: Return to Work and Modified Duties

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Return-to-work planning should restore function safely while avoiding both premature full exposure and unnecessary work absence.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Worker recovering from injury or illness
- Need for temporary restrictions, reduced hours, or altered tasks
- Concern about re-injury or inadequate tolerance for full duties
- Need for coordination between worker, supervisor, and clinician

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Modified duties should be meaningful, safe, and consistent with the worker's current tolerance
- A plan should include review points and clear progression criteria
- Psychosocial barriers, job conflict, and fear of re-injury can slow recovery even when tissues are improving

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Identify unsafe tasks, key restrictions, and whether the worker can remain productive with adjustments
- Escalate for unresolved red flags, unstable symptoms, or lack of safe duties
- Provide short, clear advice rather than vague work excuses

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Build a graded plan covering hours, lifting limits, driving, climbing, kneeling, repetitive work, and environmental exposure where relevant
- Set progression triggers based on function and tolerance rather than date alone
- Liaise with medical, rehab, and workplace stakeholders where appropriate to keep the plan realistic and safe

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Return to work is usually part of treatment, but only when the duties match the worker's current capacity
